The Fog

Part three: morning sun, a stand of pines, and ground mist. The shafts of light slanting between the crowns are not drawn — they are fog that the sun reaches, standing next to fog that it doesn't. Move your cursor through the scene to breathe more of it.

Scroll down to read ↓

Part two ended with a confession. Light in that world was binary: a ray either travelled free or stopped dead. Nothing in between — no haze, no glow trapped in smoke, no shafts slanting through the trees. Real air is the in-between. Every meter of fog a ray crosses takes a small tax, and everything this part adds follows from making that tax precise: participating media, the rendering term for any volume that meddles with light passing through it.

The machinery is unchanged — the same distance field, cascades, and merge as parts one and two. Fog enters as one new texture and two lines inside the ray march. That economy is the point: absorption and scattering are not new algorithms, they are new terms in the question every ray was already asking.

Step 1 · ExtinctionLight dies by degrees

Send a ray through fog of density ρ. In each short stretch Δs, a fixed fraction of whatever light remains is absorbed or knocked off course — fog doesn't subtract light, it taxes it proportionally. A fixed fraction per step is a decaying exponential by the time you integrate it, a law every physics student meets as Beer–Lambert: 

transmittance *= exp(−σ · ρ · Δs)   // each segment keeps a fraction, never a fixed amount

The cascade march from part one needs almost nothing else. Each ray already leaps through the scene in segments, sized by the distance field; now every leap also samples the fog texture and multiplies a running transmittance down. Whatever the ray eventually finds — a hot surface, the merge with a coarser cascade, the sky — arrives dimmed by the fog crossed on the way. The hierarchy composes correctly by itself: each cascade attenuates only its own interval, and the merge multiplies the rest in.

Slide scatter to zero first. That is pure extinction: thicken the fog and the room simply dims, the far wall fading before the near one, the lamp's shadows softening into murk — smoked glass, not weather. Something is clearly missing: real fog around a lamp glows. The light being taken from the beam has to go somewhere, and so far we have only removed it from the world. (With scatter up, look closely at the halo and you can see the machinery's grain: faint beads where the lowest cascades' few directions undersample a small bright source, and a soft comet tail when the lamp moves — the glow is last frame's light, and glowing fog magnifies every piece of bookkeeping that was invisible when only walls were lit.)

Step 2 · In-scatterWhere the stolen light goes

Extinction is really two fates. Some light is absorbed — gone to heat — but in fog most of it is scattered: knocked into a new direction, off some other droplet, into some other ray. Which means every parcel of lit fog is itself a faint light source, re-emitting a share of whatever light falls through it. To make fog glow, a ray must collect that share from every parcel it crosses: 

glow(x) = ρ(x) · fluence_last frame(x)        // how much light is in this parcel of fog…
ray += transmittance · glow · scatter · Δs   // …and the ray collects it, still subject to the tax

Look at the first line. "How much light falls on this point?" is precisely the question the cascades answer — one frame ago. This is part two's time loop again, pointed at air instead of walls: the fog's glow is last frame's fluence, the cascades then transport that glow, and next frame's fog glows with the result. Surfaces that re-emit light gave us multi-bounce for a texture read; fog that re-emits light gives us multiple scattering for the same price, one scattering order per frame, converging geometrically just like the bounce did.

Aside · Crepuscular rays

The shafts have a liturgical name — crepuscular rays, from the Latin for twilight — and a humbling explanation: nothing is being emitted along the beam at all. The sun lights all the fog it can reach; pillars and clouds deny it some volumes; you see the boundary between glowing and non-glowing air. A god ray is a shadow, viewed from the side.

Sun through two slits in a wall. The shafts are emergent: the sky from part two pours in through the gaps, the fog it reaches glows by the equation above, and the fog in the wall's shadow stays dark. Drag the sun lower and watch the shafts swing and redden; thicken the fog and they brighten while the room behind them dims — the same σ doing both jobs, since the fog that scatters light toward you is also the fog taxing everything else. Blow smoke into a shaft with your cursor and your puff lights up only while it is inside the beam.

Deep dive: the phase function we left out

Our fog re-emits equally in all directions — an isotropic phase function. Real droplets strongly prefer to deflect light just a few degrees off its original course, which is why looking toward a low sun through mist is blinding while looking away is merely grey. The standard model is the Henyey–Greenstein function, one parameter g for how forward-biased the scattering is, published in 1941 for interstellar dust. The cascades could carry it — the glow texture would need to store direction as well as magnitude — but flat-land fog earns little from it, and the isotropic version keeps the glow to one texture read. Part five returns to this kind of compromise with much sharper tools.

Step 3 · AssemblyA sunrise with weather in it

Everything at once: the dawn sky is the topmost cascade's merge exactly as in part two, the mist is densest at the ground and thins with height (one exponential, like a real atmosphere), and a drifting noise field keeps it patchy so the shafts flicker as gaps blow past. The trees are bare for a reason — winter branches are mostly holes, and holes are where god rays live.

The debug views have grown one entry. Fog density shows the media texture itself: the mist bank, your smoke puffs, the clear air above. Flip between it and final and you can see the division of labor — density is authored, glow is computed, and the same fog bank reads as luminous gold in the sun's corridor and flat grey two trunks to the right. Light only is worth a long look too: with media on, the air itself carries a value at every pixel, the first time in this series that empty space has been anything but black.

CodaThe flat world is finished

Count what the renderer now handles: emitters in any number, occluders, colored multi-bounce, an environment, and air that absorbs and scatters. That is the full cast of light transport — there is nothing qualitatively missing anymore. In two dimensions.

And that is the confession this part ends on. Every scene so far is a floor plan; light roams the plane and the camera floats politely outside it, immune to the fog, because the fog has no thickness in the third direction. Going to 3D the obvious way — voxels for the distance field, cascades over a volume — multiplies every texture in this series by a third axis. The next part takes a different road: it throws away the textures entirely and keeps the one idea that made everything here fast. The distance field stops being something we store and becomes something we evaluate — a function, sampled by rays, in three dimensions, with shadows and depth and air you can finally stand in.

Light in a Browser Tab, part three · part one: The Lava Lamp · part two: The Bonfire

Standing on the shoulders of: Sannikov, "Radiance Cascades" (2023) · Max, "Optical Models for Direct Volume Rendering" (1995) · Henyey & Greenstein, "Diffuse Radiation in the Galaxy" (1941) · Beer, "Bestimmung der Absorption des rothen Lichts in farbigen Flüssigkeiten" (1852) · Jarosz, "Efficient Monte Carlo Methods for Light Transport in Scattering Media" (2008)
